Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-build--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-build---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-build---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo build -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo build -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

[`--future-incompat-report`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-build---future-incompat-report)

Displays a future-incompat report for any future-incompatible warnings
produced during execution of this command

See [cargo-report(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-report1)

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Build the local package and all of its dependencies:


```

cargo build
```

2. Build with optimizations:


```

cargo build --release
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-rustc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-rustc1)

# cargo-check(1)

## NAME

cargo-check — Check the current package

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo check` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

Check a local package and all of its dependencies for errors. This will
essentially compile the packages without performing the final step of code
generation, which is faster than running `cargo build`. The compiler will save
metadata files to disk so that future runs will reuse them if the source has
not been modified. Some diagnostics and errors are only emitted during code
generation, so they inherently won’t be reported with `cargo check`.

## OPTIONS

### Package Selection

By default, when no package selection options are given, the packages selected
depend on the selected manifest file (based on the current working directory if
`--manifest-path` is not given). If the manifest is the root of a workspace then
the workspaces default members are selected, otherwise only the package defined
by the manifest will be selected.

The default members of a workspace can be set explicitly with the
`workspace.default-members` key in the root manifest. If this is not set, a
virtual workspace will include all workspace members (equivalent to passing
`--workspace`), and a non-virtual workspace will include only the root crate itself.

[`-p` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--p)[`--package` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---package)

Check only the specified packages. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the
SPEC format. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports common Unix
glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell accidentally
expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use single quotes or
double quotes around each pattern.

[`--workspace`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---workspace)

Check all members in the workspace.

[`--all`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---all)

Deprecated alias for `--workspace`.

[`--exclude` _SPEC_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---exclude)

Exclude the specified packages. Must be used in conjunction with the
`--workspace` flag. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports
common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell
accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use
single quotes or double quotes around each pattern.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo check` will check all
binary and library targets of the selected packages. Binaries are skipped if
they have `required-features` that are missing.

Passing target selection flags will check only the specified
targets.

Note that `--bin`, `--example`, `--test` and `--bench` flags also
support common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your
shell accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must
use single quotes or double quotes around each glob pattern.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---lib)

Check the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---bin)

Check the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---bins)

Check all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---example)

Check the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---examples)

Check all example targets.

[`--test` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---test)

Check the specified integration test. This flag may be specified
multiple times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--tests`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---tests)

Check all targets that have the `test = true` manifest
flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built as
unittests, and integration tests. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
unittest, and once as a dependency for binaries, integration tests, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `test` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--bench` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---bench)

Check the specified benchmark. This flag may be specified multiple
times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--benches`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---benches)

Check all targets that have the `bench = true`
manifest flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built
as benchmarks, and bench targets. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
benchmark, and once as a dependency for binaries, benchmarks, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `bench` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--all-targets`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---all-targets)

Check all targets. This is equivalent to specifying `--lib --bins --tests --benches --examples`.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---target)

Check for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---release)

Check optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---profile)

Check with the given profile.

As a special case, specifying the `test` profile will also enable checking in
test mode which will enable checking tests and enable the `test` cfg option.
See [rustc tests](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html) for more
detail.

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo check -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo check -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

[`--future-incompat-report`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-check---future-incompat-report)

Displays a future-incompat report for any future-incompatible warnings
produced during execution of this command

See [cargo-report(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-report1)

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Check the local package for errors:


```

cargo check
```

2. Check all targets, including unit tests:


```

cargo check --all-targets --profile=test
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-build(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-build1)

# cargo-clean(1)

## NAME

cargo-clean — Remove generated artifacts

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo clean` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

Remove artifacts from the target directory that Cargo has generated in the
past.

With no options, `cargo clean` will delete the entire target directory.

## OPTIONS

### Package Selection

When no packages are selected, all packages and all dependencies in the
workspace are cleaned.

[`-p` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--p)[`--package` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---package)

Clean only the specified packages. This flag may be specified
multiple times. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the SPEC format.

[`--workspace`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---workspace)

Clean artifacts of the workspace members.

### Clean Options

[`--dry-run`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---dry-run)

Displays a summary of what would be deleted without deleting anything.
Use with `--verbose` to display the actual files that would be deleted.

[`--doc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---doc)

This option will cause `cargo clean` to remove only the `doc` directory in
the target directory.

[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---release)

Remove all artifacts in the `release` directory.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---profile)

Remove all artifacts in the directory with the given profile name.

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---target)

Clean for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-clean--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Remove the entire target directory:


```

cargo clean
```

2. Remove only the release artifacts:


```

cargo clean --release
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-build(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-build1)

# cargo-clippy(1)

## NAME

cargo-clippy — Checks a package to catch common mistakes and improve your Rust code

## DESCRIPTION

This is an external command distributed with the Rust toolchain as an optional component.
It is not built into Cargo, and may require additional installation.

For information about usage and installation,
see [Clippy Documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/clippy/index.html).

## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1),
[cargo-fix(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fix1),
[cargo-fmt(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fmt1),
[Custom subcommands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#custom-subcommands)

# cargo-doc(1)

## NAME

cargo-doc — Build a package’s documentation

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo doc` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

Build the documentation for the local package and all dependencies. The output
is placed in `target/doc` in rustdoc’s usual format.

**Note:** Documentation generation is cumulative: existing doc files in the target directory are preserved across different `cargo doc` invocations. To remove existing generated docs, pass `--doc` to [cargo-clean(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-clean1).

## OPTIONS

### Documentation Options

[`--open`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---open)

Open the docs in a browser after building them. This will use your default
browser unless you define another one in the `BROWSER` environment variable
or use the [`doc.browser`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#docbrowser) configuration
option.

[`--no-deps`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---no-deps)

Do not build documentation for dependencies.

[`--document-private-items`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---document-private-items)

Include non-public items in the documentation. This will be enabled by default if documenting a binary target.

### Package Selection

By default, when no package selection options are given, the packages selected
depend on the selected manifest file (based on the current working directory if
`--manifest-path` is not given). If the manifest is the root of a workspace then
the workspaces default members are selected, otherwise only the package defined
by the manifest will be selected.

The default members of a workspace can be set explicitly with the
`workspace.default-members` key in the root manifest. If this is not set, a
virtual workspace will include all workspace members (equivalent to passing
`--workspace`), and a non-virtual workspace will include only the root crate itself.

[`-p` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--p)[`--package` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---package)

Document only the specified packages. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the
SPEC format. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports common Unix
glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell accidentally
expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use single quotes or
double quotes around each pattern.

[`--workspace`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---workspace)

Document all members in the workspace.

[`--all`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---all)

Deprecated alias for `--workspace`.

[`--exclude` _SPEC_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---exclude)

Exclude the specified packages. Must be used in conjunction with the
`--workspace` flag. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports
common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell
accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use
single quotes or double quotes around each pattern.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo doc` will document all
binary and library targets of the selected package. The binary will be skipped
if its name is the same as the lib target. Binaries are skipped if they have
`required-features` that are missing.

The default behavior can be changed by setting `doc = false` for the target in
the manifest settings. Using target selection options will ignore the `doc`
flag and will always document the given target.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---lib)

Document the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---bin)

Document the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---bins)

Document all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---example)

Document the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---examples)

Document all example targets.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---target)

Document for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---release)

Document optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---profile)

Document with the given profile.
See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-doc---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo doc -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo doc -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Build the local package documentation and its dependencies and output to
`target/doc`.


```

cargo doc
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-rustdoc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-rustdoc1), [rustdoc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/index.html)

# cargo-fetch(1)

## NAME

cargo-fetch — Fetch dependencies of a package from the network

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo fetch` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

If a `Cargo.lock` file is available, this command will ensure that all of the
git dependencies and/or registry dependencies are downloaded and locally
available. Subsequent Cargo commands will be able to run offline after a `cargo fetch` unless the lock file changes.

If the lock file is not available, then this command will generate the lock
file before fetching the dependencies.

If `--target` is not specified, then all target dependencies are fetched.

See also the [cargo-prefetch](https://crates.io/crates/cargo-prefetch)
plugin which adds a command to download popular crates. This may be useful if
you plan to use Cargo without a network with the `--offline` flag.

## OPTIONS

### Fetch options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---target)

Fetch for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is all architectures. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fetch--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Fetch all dependencies:


```

cargo fetch
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-update(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-update1), [cargo-generate-lockfile(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-generate-lockfile1)

# cargo-fix(1)

## NAME

cargo-fix — Automatically fix lint warnings reported by rustc

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo fix` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

This Cargo subcommand will automatically take rustc’s suggestions from
diagnostics like warnings and apply them to your source code. This is intended
to help automate tasks that rustc itself already knows how to tell you to fix!

Executing `cargo fix` will under the hood execute [cargo-check(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-check1). Any warnings
applicable to your crate will be automatically fixed (if possible) and all
remaining warnings will be displayed when the check process is finished. For
example if you’d like to apply all fixes to the current package, you can run:

```

cargo fix
```

which behaves the same as `cargo check --all-targets`.

`cargo fix` is only capable of fixing code that is normally compiled with
`cargo check`. If code is conditionally enabled with optional features, you
will need to enable those features for that code to be analyzed:

```

cargo fix --features foo
```

Similarly, other `cfg` expressions like platform-specific code will need to
pass `--target` to fix code for the given target.

```

cargo fix --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
```

If you encounter any problems with `cargo fix` or otherwise have any questions
or feature requests please don’t hesitate to file an issue at
[https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo).

### Edition migration

The `cargo fix` subcommand can also be used to migrate a package from one
[edition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/edition-guide/editions/transitioning-an-existing-project-to-a-new-edition.html) to the next. The general procedure is:

1. Run `cargo fix --edition`. Consider also using the `--all-features` flag if
your project has multiple features. You may also want to run `cargo fix --edition` multiple times with different `--target` flags if your project
has platform-specific code gated by `cfg` attributes.
2. Modify `Cargo.toml` to set the [edition field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#the-edition-field) to the new edition.
3. Run your project tests to verify that everything still works. If new
warnings are issued, you may want to consider running `cargo fix` again
(without the `--edition` flag) to apply any suggestions given by the
compiler.

And hopefully that’s it! Just keep in mind of the caveats mentioned above that
`cargo fix` cannot update code for inactive features or `cfg` expressions.
Also, in some rare cases the compiler is unable to automatically migrate all
code to the new edition, and this may require manual changes after building
with the new edition.

## OPTIONS

### Fix options

[`--broken-code`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---broken-code)

Fix code even if it already has compiler errors. This is useful if `cargo fix`
fails to apply the changes. It will apply the changes and leave the broken
code in the working directory for you to inspect and manually fix.

[`--edition`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---edition)

Apply changes that will update the code to the next edition. This will not
update the edition in the `Cargo.toml` manifest, which must be updated
manually after `cargo fix --edition` has finished.

[`--edition-idioms`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---edition-idioms)

Apply suggestions that will update code to the preferred style for the current
edition.

[`--allow-no-vcs`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---allow-no-vcs)

Fix code even if a VCS was not detected.

[`--allow-dirty`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---allow-dirty)

Fix code even if the working directory has changes (including staged changes).

[`--allow-staged`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---allow-staged)

Fix code even if the working directory has staged changes.

### Package Selection

By default, when no package selection options are given, the packages selected
depend on the selected manifest file (based on the current working directory if
`--manifest-path` is not given). If the manifest is the root of a workspace then
the workspaces default members are selected, otherwise only the package defined
by the manifest will be selected.

The default members of a workspace can be set explicitly with the
`workspace.default-members` key in the root manifest. If this is not set, a
virtual workspace will include all workspace members (equivalent to passing
`--workspace`), and a non-virtual workspace will include only the root crate itself.

[`-p` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--p)[`--package` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---package)

Fix only the specified packages. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the
SPEC format. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports common Unix
glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell accidentally
expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use single quotes or
double quotes around each pattern.

[`--workspace`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---workspace)

Fix all members in the workspace.

[`--all`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---all)

Deprecated alias for `--workspace`.

[`--exclude` _SPEC_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---exclude)

Exclude the specified packages. Must be used in conjunction with the
`--workspace` flag. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports
common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell
accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use
single quotes or double quotes around each pattern.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo fix` will fix all targets
(`--all-targets` implied). Binaries are skipped if they have
`required-features` that are missing.

Passing target selection flags will fix only the specified
targets.

Note that `--bin`, `--example`, `--test` and `--bench` flags also
support common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your
shell accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must
use single quotes or double quotes around each glob pattern.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---lib)

Fix the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---bin)

Fix the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---bins)

Fix all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---example)

Fix the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---examples)

Fix all example targets.

[`--test` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---test)

Fix the specified integration test. This flag may be specified
multiple times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--tests`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---tests)

Fix all targets that have the `test = true` manifest
flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built as
unittests, and integration tests. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
unittest, and once as a dependency for binaries, integration tests, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `test` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--bench` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---bench)

Fix the specified benchmark. This flag may be specified multiple
times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--benches`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---benches)

Fix all targets that have the `bench = true`
manifest flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built
as benchmarks, and bench targets. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
benchmark, and once as a dependency for binaries, benchmarks, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `bench` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--all-targets`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---all-targets)

Fix all targets. This is equivalent to specifying `--lib --bins --tests --benches --examples`.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---target)

Fix for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---release)

Fix optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---profile)

Fix with the given profile.

As a special case, specifying the `test` profile will also enable checking in
test mode which will enable checking tests and enable the `test` cfg option.
See [rustc tests](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html) for more
detail.

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-fix---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo fix -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo fix -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Apply compiler suggestions to the local package:


```

cargo fix
```

2. Update a package to prepare it for the next edition:


```

cargo fix --edition
```

3. Apply suggested idioms for the current edition:


```

cargo fix --edition-idioms
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-check(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-check1)

# cargo-fmt(1)

## NAME

cargo-fmt — Formats all bin and lib files of the current crate using rustfmt

## DESCRIPTION

This is an external command distributed with the Rust toolchain as an optional component.
It is not built into Cargo, and may require additional installation.

For information about usage and installation,
see [https://github.com/rust-lang/rustfmt](https://github.com/rust-lang/rustfmt).

## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1),
[cargo-fix(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fix1),
[cargo-clippy(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-clippy1),
[Custom subcommands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#custom-subcommands)

# cargo-miri(1)

## NAME

cargo-miri — Runs binary crates and tests in Miri

## DESCRIPTION

This is an external command distributed with the Rust toolchain as an optional component.
It is not built into Cargo, and may require additional installation.

This command is only available on the [nightly](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) channel.

For information about usage and installation,
see [https://github.com/rust-lang/miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri).

## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1),
[cargo-run(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-run1),
[cargo-test(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-test1),
[Custom subcommands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#custom-subcommands)

# cargo-run(1)

## NAME

cargo-run — Run the current package

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo run` \[ _options_\] \[`--` _args_\]

## DESCRIPTION

Run a binary or example of the local package.

All the arguments following the two dashes (`--`) are passed to the binary to
run. If you’re passing arguments to both Cargo and the binary, the ones after
`--` go to the binary, the ones before go to Cargo.

Unlike [cargo-test(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-test1) and [cargo-bench(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-bench1), `cargo run` sets the
working directory of the binary executed to the current working directory, same
as if it was executed in the shell directly.

## OPTIONS

### Package Selection

By default, the package in the current working directory is selected. The `-p`
flag can be used to choose a different package in a workspace.

[`-p` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--p)[`--package` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---package)

The package to run. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the SPEC
format.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo run` will run the binary
target. If there are multiple binary targets, you must pass a target flag to
choose one. Or, the `default-run` field may be specified in the `[package]`
section of `Cargo.toml` to choose the name of the binary to run by default.

[`--bin` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---bin)

Run the specified binary.

[`--example` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---example)

Run the specified example.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---target)

Run for the specified target architecture. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---release)

Run optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---profile)

Run with the given profile.
See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
