`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-run---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo run -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo run -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Build the local package and run its main target (assuming only one binary):


```

cargo run
```

2. Run an example with extra arguments:


```

cargo run --example exname -- --exoption exarg1 exarg2
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-build(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-build1)

# cargo-rustc(1)

## NAME

cargo-rustc — Compile the current package, and pass extra options to the compiler

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo rustc` \[ _options_\] \[`--` _args_\]

## DESCRIPTION

The specified target for the current package (or package specified by `-p` if
provided) will be compiled along with all of its dependencies. The specified
_args_ will all be passed to the final compiler invocation, not any of the
dependencies. Note that the compiler will still unconditionally receive
arguments such as `-L`, `--extern`, and `--crate-type`, and the specified
_args_ will simply be added to the compiler invocation.

See [https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/index.html](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/index.html) for documentation on rustc
flags.

This command requires that only one target is being compiled when additional
arguments are provided. If more than one target is available for the current
package the filters of `--lib`, `--bin`, etc, must be used to select which
target is compiled.

To pass flags to all compiler processes spawned by Cargo, use the `RUSTFLAGS` [environment variable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) or the
`build.rustflags` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

## OPTIONS

### Package Selection

By default, the package in the current working directory is selected. The `-p`
flag can be used to choose a different package in a workspace.

[`-p` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--p)[`--package` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---package)

The package to build. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the SPEC
format.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo rustc` will build all
binary and library targets of the selected package.

Binary targets are automatically built if there is an integration test or
benchmark being selected to build. This allows an integration
test to execute the binary to exercise and test its behavior.
The `CARGO_BIN_EXE_<name>` [environment variable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-cargo-sets-for-crates)
is set when the integration test is built and run so that it can use the
[`env` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.env.html) or the
[`var` function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/env/fn.var.html) to locate the
executable.

Passing target selection flags will build only the specified
targets.

Note that `--bin`, `--example`, `--test` and `--bench` flags also
support common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your
shell accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must
use single quotes or double quotes around each glob pattern.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---lib)

Build the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---bin)

Build the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---bins)

Build all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---example)

Build the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---examples)

Build all example targets.

[`--test` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---test)

Build the specified integration test. This flag may be specified
multiple times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--tests`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---tests)

Build all targets that have the `test = true` manifest
flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built as
unittests, and integration tests. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
unittest, and once as a dependency for binaries, integration tests, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `test` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--bench` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---bench)

Build the specified benchmark. This flag may be specified multiple
times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--benches`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---benches)

Build all targets that have the `bench = true`
manifest flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built
as benchmarks, and bench targets. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
benchmark, and once as a dependency for binaries, benchmarks, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `bench` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--all-targets`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---all-targets)

Build all targets. This is equivalent to specifying `--lib --bins --tests --benches --examples`.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---target)

Build for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---release)

Build optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---profile)

Build with the given profile.

The `rustc` subcommand will treat the following named profiles with special behaviors:

- `check` — Builds in the same way as the [cargo-check(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-check1) command with
the `dev` profile.
- `test` — Builds in the same way as the [cargo-test(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-test1) command,
enabling building in test mode which will enable tests and enable the `test`
cfg option. See [rustc\\
tests](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html) for more detail.
- `bench` — Builds in the same was as the [cargo-bench(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-bench1) command,
similar to the `test` profile.

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

[`--crate-type` _crate-type_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---crate-type)

Build for the given crate type. This flag accepts a comma-separated list of
1 or more crate types, of which the allowed values are the same as `crate-type`
field in the manifest for configuring a Cargo target. See
[`crate-type` field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#the-crate-type-field)
for possible values.

If the manifest contains a list, and `--crate-type` is provided,
the command-line argument value will override what is in the manifest.

This flag only works when building a `lib` or `example` library target.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo rustc -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo rustc -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

[`--future-incompat-report`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustc---future-incompat-report)

Displays a future-incompat report for any future-incompatible warnings
produced during execution of this command

See [cargo-report(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-report1)

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Check if your package (not including dependencies) uses unsafe code:


```

cargo rustc --lib -- -D unsafe-code
```

2. Try an experimental flag on the nightly compiler, such as this which prints
the size of every type:


```

cargo rustc --lib -- -Z print-type-sizes
```

3. Override `crate-type` field in Cargo.toml with command-line option:


```

cargo rustc --lib --crate-type lib,cdylib
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-build(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-build1), [rustc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/index.html)

# cargo-rustdoc(1)

## NAME

cargo-rustdoc — Build a package’s documentation, using specified custom flags

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo rustdoc` \[ _options_\] \[`--` _args_\]

## DESCRIPTION

The specified target for the current package (or package specified by `-p` if
provided) will be documented with the specified _args_ being passed to the
final rustdoc invocation. Dependencies will not be documented as part of this
command. Note that rustdoc will still unconditionally receive arguments such
as `-L`, `--extern`, and `--crate-type`, and the specified _args_ will simply
be added to the rustdoc invocation.

See [https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/index.html](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/index.html) for documentation on rustdoc
flags.

This command requires that only one target is being compiled when additional
arguments are provided. If more than one target is available for the current
package the filters of `--lib`, `--bin`, etc, must be used to select which
target is compiled.

To pass flags to all rustdoc processes spawned by Cargo, use the
`RUSTDOCFLAGS` [environment variable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1)
or the `build.rustdocflags` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

## OPTIONS

### Documentation Options

[`--open`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---open)

Open the docs in a browser after building them. This will use your default
browser unless you define another one in the `BROWSER` environment variable
or use the [`doc.browser`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#docbrowser) configuration
option.

### Package Selection

By default, the package in the current working directory is selected. The `-p`
flag can be used to choose a different package in a workspace.

[`-p` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--p)[`--package` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---package)

The package to document. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the SPEC
format.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo rustdoc` will document all
binary and library targets of the selected package. The binary will be skipped
if its name is the same as the lib target. Binaries are skipped if they have
`required-features` that are missing.

Passing target selection flags will document only the specified
targets.

Note that `--bin`, `--example`, `--test` and `--bench` flags also
support common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your
shell accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must
use single quotes or double quotes around each glob pattern.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---lib)

Document the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---bin)

Document the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---bins)

Document all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---example)

Document the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---examples)

Document all example targets.

[`--test` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---test)

Document the specified integration test. This flag may be specified
multiple times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--tests`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---tests)

Document all targets that have the `test = true` manifest
flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built as
unittests, and integration tests. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
unittest, and once as a dependency for binaries, integration tests, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `test` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--bench` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---bench)

Document the specified benchmark. This flag may be specified multiple
times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--benches`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---benches)

Document all targets that have the `bench = true`
manifest flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built
as benchmarks, and bench targets. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
benchmark, and once as a dependency for binaries, benchmarks, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `bench` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--all-targets`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---all-targets)

Document all targets. This is equivalent to specifying `--lib --bins --tests --benches --examples`.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---target)

Document for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---release)

Document optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---profile)

Document with the given profile.
See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--keep-going`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---keep-going)

Build as many crates in the dependency graph as possible, rather than aborting
the build on the first one that fails to build.

For example if the current package depends on dependencies `fails` and `works`,
one of which fails to build, `cargo rustdoc -j1` may or may not build the
one that succeeds (depending on which one of the two builds Cargo picked to run
first), whereas `cargo rustdoc -j1 --keep-going` would definitely run both
builds, even if the one run first fails.

[`--output-format`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-rustdoc---output-format)

The output type for the documentation emitted. Valid values:

- `html` (default): Emit the documentation in HTML format.
- `json`: Emit the documentation in the [experimental JSON format](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustdoc_json_types).

This option is only available on the [nightly channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html)
and requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Build documentation with custom CSS included from a given file:


```

cargo rustdoc --lib -- --extend-css extra.css
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-doc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-doc1), [rustdoc(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/index.html)

# cargo-test(1)

## NAME

cargo-test — Execute unit and integration tests of a package

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo test` \[ _options_\] \[ _testname_\] \[`--` _test-options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

Compile and execute unit, integration, and documentation tests.

The test filtering argument `TESTNAME` and all the arguments following the two
dashes (`--`) are passed to the test binaries and thus to _libtest_ (rustc’s
built in unit-test and micro-benchmarking framework). If you’re passing
arguments to both Cargo and the binary, the ones after `--` go to the binary,
the ones before go to Cargo. For details about libtest’s arguments see the
output of `cargo test -- --help` and check out the rustc book’s chapter on
how tests work at [https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html).

As an example, this will filter for tests with `foo` in their name and run them
on 3 threads in parallel:

```

cargo test foo -- --test-threads 3
```

Tests are built with the `--test` option to `rustc` which creates a special
executable by linking your code with libtest. The executable automatically
runs all functions annotated with the `#[test]` attribute in multiple threads.
`#[bench]` annotated functions will also be run with one iteration to verify
that they are functional.

If the package contains multiple test targets, each target compiles to a
special executable as aforementioned, and then is run serially.

The libtest harness may be disabled by setting `harness = false` in the target
manifest settings, in which case your code will need to provide its own `main`
function to handle running tests.

### Documentation tests

Documentation tests are also run by default, which is handled by `rustdoc`. It
extracts code samples from documentation comments of the library target, and
then executes them.

Different from normal test targets, each code block compiles to a doctest
executable on the fly with `rustc`. These executables run in parallel in
separate processes. The compilation of a code block is in fact a part of test
function controlled by libtest, so some options such as `--jobs` might not
take effect. Note that this execution model of doctests is not guaranteed
and may change in the future; beware of depending on it.

See the [rustdoc book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/) for more information
on writing doc tests.

### Working directory of tests

The working directory when running each unit and integration test is set to the
root directory of the package the test belongs to.
Setting the working directory of tests to the package’s root directory makes it
possible for tests to reliably access the package’s files using relative paths,
regardless from where `cargo test` was executed from.

For documentation tests, the working directory when invoking `rustdoc` is set to
the workspace root directory, and is also the directory `rustdoc` uses as the
compilation directory of each documentation test.
The working directory when running each documentation test is set to the root
directory of the package the test belongs to, and is controlled via `rustdoc`’s
`--test-run-directory` option.

## OPTIONS

### Test Options

[`--no-run`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---no-run)

Compile, but don’t run tests.

[`--no-fail-fast`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---no-fail-fast)

Run all tests regardless of failure. Without this flag, Cargo will exit
after the first executable fails. The Rust test harness will run all tests
within the executable to completion, this flag only applies to the executable
as a whole.

### Package Selection

By default, when no package selection options are given, the packages selected
depend on the selected manifest file (based on the current working directory if
`--manifest-path` is not given). If the manifest is the root of a workspace then
the workspaces default members are selected, otherwise only the package defined
by the manifest will be selected.

The default members of a workspace can be set explicitly with the
`workspace.default-members` key in the root manifest. If this is not set, a
virtual workspace will include all workspace members (equivalent to passing
`--workspace`), and a non-virtual workspace will include only the root crate itself.

[`-p` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--p)[`--package` _spec_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---package)

Test only the specified packages. See [cargo-pkgid(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1) for the
SPEC format. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports common Unix
glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell accidentally
expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use single quotes or
double quotes around each pattern.

[`--workspace`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---workspace)

Test all members in the workspace.

[`--all`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---all)

Deprecated alias for `--workspace`.

[`--exclude` _SPEC_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---exclude)

Exclude the specified packages. Must be used in conjunction with the
`--workspace` flag. This flag may be specified multiple times and supports
common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your shell
accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must use
single quotes or double quotes around each pattern.

### Target Selection

When no target selection options are given, `cargo test` will build the
following targets of the selected packages:

- lib — used to link with binaries, examples, integration tests, and doc tests
- bins (only if integration tests are built and required features are
available)
- examples — to ensure they compile
- lib as a unit test
- bins as unit tests
- integration tests
- doc tests for the lib target

The default behavior can be changed by setting the `test` flag for the target
in the manifest settings. Setting examples to `test = true` will build and run
the example as a test, replacing the example’s `main` function with the
libtest harness. If you don’t want the `main` function replaced, also include
`harness = false`, in which case the example will be built and executed as-is.

Setting targets to `test = false` will stop them from being tested by default.
Target selection options that take a target by name (such as `--example foo`)
ignore the `test` flag and will always test the given target.

Doc tests for libraries may be disabled by setting `doctest = false` for the
library in the manifest.

See [Configuring a target](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuring-a-target)
for more information on per-target settings.

Binary targets are automatically built if there is an integration test or
benchmark being selected to test. This allows an integration
test to execute the binary to exercise and test its behavior.
The `CARGO_BIN_EXE_<name>` [environment variable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-cargo-sets-for-crates)
is set when the integration test is built and run so that it can use the
[`env` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.env.html) or the
[`var` function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/env/fn.var.html) to locate the
executable.

Passing target selection flags will test only the specified
targets.

Note that `--bin`, `--example`, `--test` and `--bench` flags also
support common Unix glob patterns like `*`, `?` and `[]`. However, to avoid your
shell accidentally expanding glob patterns before Cargo handles them, you must
use single quotes or double quotes around each glob pattern.

[`--lib`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---lib)

Test the package’s library.

[`--bin` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---bin)

Test the specified binary. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--bins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---bins)

Test all binary targets.

[`--example` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---example)

Test the specified example. This flag may be specified multiple times
and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--examples`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---examples)

Test all example targets.

[`--test` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---test)

Test the specified integration test. This flag may be specified
multiple times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--tests`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---tests)

Test all targets that have the `test = true` manifest
flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built as
unittests, and integration tests. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
unittest, and once as a dependency for binaries, integration tests, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `test` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--bench` _name_…](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---bench)

Test the specified benchmark. This flag may be specified multiple
times and supports common Unix glob patterns.

[`--benches`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---benches)

Test all targets that have the `bench = true`
manifest flag set. By default this includes the library and binaries built
as benchmarks, and bench targets. Be aware that this will also build any
required dependencies, so the lib target may be built twice (once as a
benchmark, and once as a dependency for binaries, benchmarks, etc.).
Targets may be enabled or disabled by setting the `bench` flag in the
manifest settings for the target.

[`--all-targets`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---all-targets)

Test all targets. This is equivalent to specifying `--lib --bins --tests --benches --examples`.

[`--doc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---doc)

Test only the library’s documentation. This cannot be mixed with other
target options.

### Feature Selection

The feature flags allow you to control which features are enabled. When no
feature options are given, the `default` feature is activated for every
selected package.

See [the features documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-feature-options)
for more details.

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---features)

Space or comma separated list of features to activate. Features of workspace
members may be enabled with `package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may
be specified multiple times, which enables all specified features.

[`--all-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---all-features)

Activate all available features of all selected packages.

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---no-default-features)

Do not activate the `default` feature of the selected packages.

### Compilation Options

[`--target` _triple_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---target)

Test for the specified target architecture. Flag may be specified multiple times. The default is the host architecture. The general format of the triple is
`<arch><sub>-<vendor>-<sys>-<abi>`.

Possible values:

- Any supported target in `rustc --print target-list`.
- `"host-tuple"`, which will internally be substituted by the host’s target. This can be particularly useful if you’re cross-compiling some crates, and don’t want to specify your host’s machine as a target (for instance, an `xtask` in a shared project that may be worked on by many hosts).
- A path to a custom target specification. See [Custom Target Lookup Path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/targets/custom.html#custom-target-lookup-path) for more information.

This may also be specified with the `build.target` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

Note that specifying this flag makes Cargo run in a different mode where the
target artifacts are placed in a separate directory. See the
[build cache](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-cache) documentation for more details.

[`-r`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--r)[`--release`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---release)

Test optimized artifacts with the `release` profile.
See also the `--profile` option for choosing a specific profile by name.

[`--profile` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---profile)

Test with the given profile.
See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#profiles) for more details on profiles.

[`--timings`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---timings)

Output information how long each compilation takes, and track concurrency
information over time.

A file `cargo-timing.html` will be written to the `target/cargo-timings`
directory at the end of the build. An additional report with a timestamp
in its filename is also written if you want to look at a previous run.
These reports are suitable for human consumption only, and do not provide
machine-readable timing data.

### Output Options

[`--target-dir` _directory_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---target-dir)

Directory for all generated artifacts and intermediate files. May also be
specified with the `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` environment variable, or the
`build.target-dir` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).
Defaults to `target` in the root of the workspace.

### Display Options

By default the Rust test harness hides output from test execution to keep
results readable. Test output can be recovered (e.g., for debugging) by passing
`--no-capture` to the test binaries:

```

cargo test -- --no-capture
```

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--message-format` _fmt_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---message-format)

The output format for diagnostic messages. Can be specified multiple times
and consists of comma-separated values. Valid values:

- `human` (default): Display in a human-readable text format. Conflicts with
`short` and `json`.
- `short`: Emit shorter, human-readable text messages. Conflicts with `human`
and `json`.
- `json`: Emit JSON messages to stdout. See
[the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#json-messages)
for more details. Conflicts with `human` and `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-short`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages contains
the “short” rendering from rustc. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-diagnostic-rendered-ansi`: Ensure the `rendered` field of JSON messages
contains embedded ANSI color codes for respecting rustc’s default color
scheme. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.
- `json-render-diagnostics`: Instruct Cargo to not include rustc diagnostics
in JSON messages printed, but instead Cargo itself should render the
JSON diagnostics coming from rustc. Cargo’s own JSON diagnostics and others
coming from rustc are still emitted. Cannot be used with `human` or `short`.

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

### Miscellaneous Options

The `--jobs` argument affects the building of the test executable but does not
affect how many threads are used when running the tests. The Rust test harness
includes an option to control the number of threads used:

```

cargo test -j 2 -- --test-threads=2
```

[`-j` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test--j)[`--jobs` _N_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---jobs)

Number of parallel jobs to run. May also be specified with the
`build.jobs` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). Defaults to
the number of logical CPUs. If negative, it sets the maximum number of
parallel jobs to the number of logical CPUs plus provided value. If
a string `default` is provided, it sets the value back to defaults.
Should not be 0.

[`--future-incompat-report`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-test---future-incompat-report)

Displays a future-incompat report for any future-incompatible warnings
produced during execution of this command

See [cargo-report(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-report1)

While `cargo test` involves compilation, it does not provide a `--keep-going`
flag. Use `--no-fail-fast` to run as many tests as possible without stopping at
the first failure. To “compile” as many tests as possible, use `--tests` to
build test binaries separately. For example:

```

cargo build --tests --keep-going
cargo test --tests --no-fail-fast
```

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Execute all the unit and integration tests of the current package:


```

cargo test
```

2. Run only tests whose names match against a filter string:


```

cargo test name_filter
```

3. Run only a specific test within a specific integration test:


```

cargo test --test int_test_name -- modname::test_name
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-bench(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-bench1), [types of tests](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#tests-1), [how to write tests](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/tests/index.html)

# Manifest Commands

- [cargo add](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-add1)
- [cargo\_info](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-info1)
- [cargo generate-lockfile](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-generate-lockfile1)
- [cargo locate-project](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-locate-project1)
- [cargo metadata](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-metadata1)
- [cargo pkgid](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-pkgid1)
- [cargo remove](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-remove1)
- [cargo tree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-tree1)
- [cargo update](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-update1)
- [cargo vendor](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-vendor1)

# cargo-add(1)

## NAME

cargo-add — Add dependencies to a Cargo.toml manifest file

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo add` \[ _options_\] _crate_…

`cargo add` \[ _options_\] `--path` _path_

`cargo add` \[ _options_\] `--git` _url_ \[ _crate_…\]

## DESCRIPTION

This command can add or modify dependencies.

The source for the dependency can be specified with:

- _crate_`@` _version_: Fetch from a registry with a version constraint of “ _version_”
- `--path` _path_: Fetch from the specified _path_
- `--git` _url_: Pull from a git repo at _url_

If no source is specified, then a best effort will be made to select one, including:

- Existing dependencies in other tables (like `dev-dependencies`)
- Workspace members
- Latest release in the registry

When you add a package that is already present, the existing entry will be updated with the flags specified.

Upon successful invocation, the enabled (`+`) and disabled (`-`) [features](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#features-1) of the specified
dependency will be listed in the command’s output.

## OPTIONS

### Source options

[`--git` _url_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---git)

[Git URL to add the specified crate from](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#specifying-dependencies-from-git-repositories).

[`--branch` _branch_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---branch)

Branch to use when adding from git.

[`--tag` _tag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---tag)

Tag to use when adding from git.

[`--rev` _sha_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---rev)

Specific commit to use when adding from git.

[`--path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---path)

[Filesystem path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#specifying-path-dependencies) to local crate to add.

[`--base` _base_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---base)

The [path base](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#path-bases) to use when adding a local crate.

[Unstable (nightly-only)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#path-bases)

[`--registry` _registry_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---registry)

Name of the registry to use. Registry names are defined in [Cargo config\\
files](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1). If not specified, the default registry is used,
which is defined by the `registry.default` config key which defaults to
`crates-io`.

### Section options

[`--dev`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---dev)

Add as a [development dependency](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#development-dependencies).

[`--build`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---build)

Add as a [build dependency](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#build-dependencies).

[`--target` _target_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---target)

Add as a dependency to the [given target platform](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#platform-specific-dependencies).

To avoid unexpected shell expansions, you may use quotes around each target, e.g., `--target 'cfg(unix)'`.

### Dependency options

[`--dry-run`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---dry-run)

Don’t actually write the manifest

[`--rename` _name_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---rename)

[Rename](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#renaming-dependencies-in-cargotoml) the dependency.

[`--optional`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---optional)

Mark the dependency as [optional](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#optional-dependencies).

[`--no-optional`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---no-optional)

Mark the dependency as [required](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#optional-dependencies).

[`--public`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---public)

Mark the dependency as public.

The dependency can be referenced in your library’s public API.

[Unstable (nightly-only)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#public-dependency)

[`--no-public`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---no-public)

Mark the dependency as private.

While you can use the crate in your implementation, it cannot be referenced in your public API.

[Unstable (nightly-only)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#public-dependency)

[`--no-default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---no-default-features)

Disable the [default features](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#dependency-features).

[`--default-features`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---default-features)

Re-enable the [default features](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#dependency-features).

[`-F` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--F)[`--features` _features_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---features)

Space or comma separated list of [features to\\
activate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#dependency-features). When adding multiple
crates, the features for a specific crate may be enabled with
`package-name/feature-name` syntax. This flag may be specified multiple times,
which enables all specified features.

### Display Options

[`-v`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--v)[`--verbose`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---verbose)

Use verbose output. May be specified twice for “very verbose” output which
includes extra output such as dependency warnings and build script output.
May also be specified with the `term.verbose` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`-q`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--q)[`--quiet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---quiet)

Do not print cargo log messages.
May also be specified with the `term.quiet` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--color` _when_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---color)

Control when colored output is used. Valid values:

- `auto` (default): Automatically detect if color support is available on the
terminal.
- `always`: Always display colors.
- `never`: Never display colors.

May also be specified with the `term.color` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

### Manifest Options

[`--manifest-path` _path_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---manifest-path)

Path to the `Cargo.toml` file. By default, Cargo searches for the
`Cargo.toml` file in the current directory or any parent directory.

[`-p` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--p)[`--package` _spec_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---package)

Add dependencies to only the specified package.

[`--ignore-rust-version`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---ignore-rust-version)

Ignore `rust-version` specification in packages.

[`--locked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---locked)

Asserts that the exact same dependencies and versions are used as when the
existing `Cargo.lock` file was originally generated. Cargo will exit with an
error when either of the following scenarios arises:

- The lock file is missing.
- Cargo attempted to change the lock file due to a different dependency resolution.

It may be used in environments where deterministic builds are desired,
such as in CI pipelines.

[`--offline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---offline)

Prevents Cargo from accessing the network for any reason. Without this
flag, Cargo will stop with an error if it needs to access the network and
the network is not available. With this flag, Cargo will attempt to
proceed without the network if possible.

Beware that this may result in different dependency resolution than online
mode. Cargo will restrict itself to crates that are downloaded locally, even
if there might be a newer version as indicated in the local copy of the index.
See the [cargo-fetch(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-fetch1) command to download dependencies before going
offline.

May also be specified with the `net.offline` [config value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#configuration-1).

[`--frozen`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---frozen)

Equivalent to specifying both `--locked` and `--offline`.

### Common Options

[`+` _toolchain_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add-+toolchain)

If Cargo has been installed with rustup, and the first argument to `cargo`
begins with `+`, it will be interpreted as a rustup toolchain name (such
as `+stable` or `+nightly`).
See the [rustup documentation](https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/overrides.html)
for more information about how toolchain overrides work.

[`--config` _KEY=VALUE_ or _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---config)

Overrides a Cargo configuration value. The argument should be in TOML syntax of `KEY=VALUE`,
or provided as a path to an extra configuration file. This flag may be specified multiple times.
See the [command-line overrides section](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#command-line-overrides) for more information.

[`-C` _PATH_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--C)

Changes the current working directory before executing any specified operations. This affects
things like where cargo looks by default for the project manifest (`Cargo.toml`), as well as
the directories searched for discovering `.cargo/config.toml`, for example. This option must
appear before the command name, for example `cargo -C path/to/my-project build`.

This option is only available on the [nightly\\
channel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-07-nightly-rust.html) and
requires the `-Z unstable-options` flag to enable (see
[#10098](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10098)).

[`-h`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--h)[`--help`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add---help)

Prints help information.

[`-Z` _flag_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#option-cargo-add--Z)

Unstable (nightly-only) flags to Cargo. Run `cargo -Z help` for details.

## ENVIRONMENT

See [the reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#environment-variables-1) for
details on environment variables that Cargo reads.

## EXIT STATUS

- `0`: Cargo succeeded.
- `101`: Cargo failed to complete.

## EXAMPLES

1. Add `regex` as a dependency


```

cargo add regex
```

2. Add `trybuild` as a dev-dependency


```

cargo add --dev trybuild
```

3. Add an older version of `nom` as a dependency


```

cargo add nom@5
```

4. Add support for serializing data structures to json with `derive`s


```

cargo add serde serde_json -F serde/derive
```

5. Add `windows` as a platform specific dependency on `cfg(windows)`


```

cargo add windows --target 'cfg(windows)'
```


## SEE ALSO

[cargo(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo1), [cargo-remove(1)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/print.html#cargo-remove1)

# cargo-generate-lockfile(1)

## NAME

cargo-generate-lockfile — Generate the lockfile for a package

## SYNOPSIS

`cargo generate-lockfile` \[ _options_\]

## DESCRIPTION

This command will create the `Cargo.lock` lockfile for the current package or
workspace. If the lockfile already exists, it will be rebuilt with the latest
available version of every package.
